coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of hierarchical porous BCN using ternary deep eutectic solvent as precursor and template for aerobic oxidative desulfurization
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Yanchen
Wu, Peiwen
Luo, Jing
Dai, Li
Li, Hongping
Zhang, Ming
Chen, Linlin
Wang, Leigang
Zhu, Wenshuai
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: Boron carbon nitride (BCN) has exhibited superior aerobic catalytic performance in oxidation reactions. Herein, the hierarchical porous BCN catalyst was synthesized using a ternary deep eutectic solvent (DES) composed of boric acid, urea and choline chloride as the precursor and template. The as-prepared BCN exhibited impressive catalytic performance in oxidative desulfurization of fuel. Under the optimized conditions, the BCN catalyst can reach 98.4% sulfur removal in 4 h and recycle 5 times with a little activity decrease. Experimental results revealed that the excellent catalytic activity was found to be originated from an improved electron delocalization. Combined with the density functional theory (DFT), the improved electron delocalization increased the electronic density of the BCN, promoting the activation of dioxygen. This strategy provides a novel approach for the design of high-performance catalyst in oxidative desulfurization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxidative desulfurization
Boron carbon nitride
Deep eutectic solvents
Hierarchical porous structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The (A) FT-IR spectra, (B) UV–vis DRS spectra and (C) XRD patterns of the as-prepared BCNs.
The images of (A) SEM and (B) TEM of the BCN-1.
The (A) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and (B) surface area distribution of the BCNs and BN.
The (A) B 1s and (B) N 1s XPS spectra of the BCN-1; the (C) B 1s and (D) N 1s XPS spectra of the BN; the (E) FT-IR spectra and (F) EPR spectra of the BCN-1 and BN.

(A) The catalytic performance of the BCN-1 and BN to the DBT model diesel at 125 °C; experimental conditions: m(cat.) = 50 mg, V(model diesel) = 20 mL, v(airflow) = 100 mL/min; (B) the cyclic voltammetry curves of the BCN-1 and BN in oxygen-saturated 0.1 mol/L KOH solutions.

(A) The catalytic efficiency of the BCNs for the removal of DBT at 125 °C; (B) the effect of reaction temperature on the catalytic efficiency of BCN-1 for the removal of DBT; (C) the catalytic efficiency of the BCN-1 for the removal of DBT derivatives at 125 °C; (D) the repeated cycle-experiment of BCN-1 for the removal of DBT at 125 °C; experimental conditions: m(cat.) = 50 mg, V(model diesel) = 20 mL, v(airflow) = 100 mL/min.
Free energy profile of the ODS reaction pathways on BN and BCN with zig-N or zig-B edge models.
The synthetic route diagram of the porous BCN catalyst.
Mechanism of enhanced oxidative desulfurization activity on BCN.
The elemental composition of the BCNs and BN.
Aerobic oxidation of DBT over various catalysts under similar conditions.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of hierarchical porous BCN using ternary deep eutectic solvent as precursor and template for aerobic oxidative desulfurization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron carbon nitride (BCN) has exhibited superior aerobic catalytic performance in oxidation reactions. Herein, the hierarchical porous BCN catalyst was synthesized using a ternary deep eutectic solvent (DES) composed of boric acid, urea and choline chloride as the precursor and template. The as-prepared BCN exhibited impressive catalytic performance in oxidative desulfurization of fuel. Under the optimized conditions, the BCN catalyst can reach 98.4% sulfur removal in 4 h and recycle 5 times with a little activity decrease. Experimental results revealed that the excellent catalytic activity was found to be originated from an improved electron delocalization. Combined with the density functional theory (DFT), the improved electron delocalization increased the electronic density of the BCN, promoting the activation of dioxygen. This strategy provides a novel approach for the design of high-performance catalyst in oxidative desulfurization.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging method to produce clean diesel, oxidative desulfurization (ODS) technology can facilely oxidize refractory sulfides to corresponding sulfones, which can be removed from diesel under mild conditions [1–7]. As the core of the ODS technology, high-performance catalysts are always required during the catalytic reactions. Up to date, many kinds of catalyst, such as metal oxides [8–10], polyoxometalates [11–13], ionic liquids [3,14,15], zeolites [16–19], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [20–25], MOFs-deriving carbons [26,27], and graphene materials [28–30], have been employed to form the ODS systems. Among them, the ODS system using metal-free catalyst and dioxygen has attracted much attention because of its vast resource and low pollution.

Hexagonal boron carbon nitride (BCN), a ceramics combined of B, C, N atoms in a two-dimensional layered structure, has been noted for its complementary properties to those of boron nitride and graphite. Due to the various distribution of elements, the electronic structure of BCN is tunable, which facilitates its application in the oxidation reaction [31–33]. Wang et al. [34] and Xiao et al. [35] reported the enhanced oxidative dehydrogenation of C–H bonds catalyzed by the BCN materials. The edge structures with unsaturated atoms are regarded to be the reactive sites of BCN in catalysis. Our group also reported the activation of dioxygen on the zigzag type N (zig-N) edge of boron nitride (BN) for the oxidative desulfurization [36] and dehydrogenation [37]. Therefore, the catalytic performance of BCN is strongly governed by the electronic structure on edges, which can be affected by the element dispersion. Unfortunately, the uniformly homogeneous composition of the BCN is hard to be generated. The carbon in the BCN tends to form the clusters resulting in the separated graphene and boron nitride domain [32]. Therefore, controllable synthesis to prevent the BCN from carbon agglomeration is vital to the generation of high-performance catalysts. In the typical synthesis [31,34], the sources of boron, carbon, and nitrogen were mixed to obtain a solid precursor, followed by the calcination to generate the BCN. Moreover, in order to get the reasonably homogeneous distribution of elements in the BCN, solvents have been employed to form a more even precursor [38]. Such a complicated synthetic route not only complexes in the synthesis of BCN but also increases the costs.

Deep eutectic solvent (DES) refers to a kind of homogeneous liquid mixture composed of multi-components which could form hydrogen bonds to lower the freezing point [39,40]. DES is an ionic liquid analogue that has low vapor pressure, excellent solubilities, designable properties, but rather a low cost [41,42]. Before this work, DES has been applied in the preparation of materials, such as zeolite [43], metal phosphates [44,45], carbon dots [46,47] and MOFs [48,49]. Therefore, the DES can also work as a great medium in the same way to obtain a homogeneous precursor of BCN. Such a homogeneous precursor can facilitate the dispersion of carbon atoms, alleviating the excessive agglomeration of carbon in the BCN. On the other hand, the DES can also serve as a template to increase the porosity of the as-prepared materials [43,48]. As the literature reported, the BCN phase can be generated from the part of the DES components at the stage of thermal polycondensation, while the remained components are segregated into the depleted phase [50–53]. Such a spinodal decomposition process of DES during calcination can promote the formation of porous structure in the BCN. It was suggested that the porous structure in the catalyst could facilitate the mass transfer between the catalyst and substrate. Until now, there have been many reports on the porous material for ODS reactions (Table S1).

In this work, the ternary DES composed of urea, choline chloride and with different ratio of boron acid were prepared. The as-prepared DESs were applied to synthesize the hierarchical porous BCN catalyst for the aerobic oxidative desulfurization (Scheme 1 ). The hierarchical porous structure contains both micropores and mesopores, which guarantee the large specific surface area and fast mass transfer for enhancing ODS performance of catalysts [54–58]. It was investigated that the porous structures of the as-prepared BCNs can be adjusted by the ratio of DES components. Moreover, compared with the BN, the carbon clusters in the BCN was found to have a promotion to the electron delocalization, accelerating the activation of dioxygen oxidant. The mechanism of the BCN catalyzed oxidation was carefully discussed to reveal the reaction pathway using density functional theory (DFT). Also, the zig-N edges of BCN were investigated to be the active sites for the oxidative desulfurization.

Dibenzothiophene (DBT, 98%), 4-methyldibenzothiophene (4-MDBT, 96%), 4,6-dimethyldibenzothiphene (4,6-DMDBT, 97%), tetradecane (C14H30, 99%) and decahydronaphthalene (C10H18, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. Boric acid (H3BO3, AR grade), urea (CH4N2O, AR grade), choline chloride (ChCl, AR grade), methanol (CH3OH, AR grade) and acetonitrile (CH3CN, AR grade) were marketed from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd.

The DESs were synthesized from urea, choline chloride and boron acid in different ratios. In a typical process, 0.3092/0.4638/0.6184 g of H3BO3, 9.009 g of urea and 6.9815 g of ChCl were mixed. Then the obtained mixture was heated to 100 °C under continuous stirring. It could be observed that the mixture gradually melted forming the homogeneous liquids, which were denoted as DES-1, DES-2 and DES-3. The as-prepared DESs were collected and stored at 45 °C.

The as-prepared DES was calcinated to obtain the BCN catalyst. For instance, 15.0 g of DES-1 was placed into a tubular furnace under an ammonia flow. Then the DES-1 was calcinated at 900 °C for 2 h. After naturally cooling down to ambient temperature, the residual solid was collected as the BCN-1 catalyst. Following the same method, the residual solids from the calcination of the DES-2 and DES-3 were collected and denoted as the BCN-2 and BCN-3, respectively.

The BN was synthesized as a comparison following our previous work. In a typical process, 0.3092 g of H3BO3 and 9.009 g of urea were mixed and dissolved in a solution composed of 20 mL of distilled water and 20 mL of methanol. The solution was stirred and heated at 60 °C for 24 h to remove the solvent. The obtained white crystal powder was collected and placed into a tubular furnace under an ammonia flow. Then the powder was calcinated at 900 °C for 2 h. After naturally cooling down to ambient temperature, the residual solid was collected as the BN catalyst.

The as-prepared catalysts were characterized by multiple characterization techniques. The X-ray diffractions (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker D8 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) employing a scanning rate of 7°/min in the 2θ range from 10° to 80°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCA-LAB250, in which the standard monochromatic Al Ka excitation was at 1486.6 eV, was utilized to determine the element valence of catalysts. The ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance (UV–vis DRS) spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectra were obtained on a Thermo Nexus 470 using KBr tablets. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was operated with an A300-10/12 (Bruker, Germany) at ambient temperature. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm data were collected on the ASAP 2020 (Micromeritics, USA). The specific surface area was calculated from the data between P/Po = 0.01 and 0.30 by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method (BET) method, and the pore size distribution was calculated by quenched solid-state functional theory (QSDFT) using the carbon slit/cylinder pores model at 77 K. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was operated with a JSM-7800 F (JEOL, Japan). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was observed on a Tecnai 12 (Philips, Holland).

A dispersion-corrected hybrid density functional (M06–2X + D3) with a 6-311 + G(d,p) basis set was used to calculate the involved energetics on the edges of catalysts by the Gaussian 16 suite of programs [59]. The M06–2X functional enable the calculation of main-group thermochemistry with broad accuracy [60]. All the coordinates were fully optimized without any imaginary frequency for the models. For an elementary reaction, the change of Gibbs free energy (ΔG) was obtained using the following formula:
(1)
Δ G =
G
P
−
G
R
where the G
P and G
R are the free energy of the product and reactant, respectively.

The aromatic sulfur component dissolved model diesel was obtained as follows. In a typical method, the DBT model diesel was prepared by dissolving the desired amount of DBT in decalin to control the S-content at 500 ppm. The 4-MDBT model diesel and 4,6-DMDBT model diesel were prepared as well to make the S-content at 500 ppm, respectively.

The catalytic activity test was carried out by mixing the catalysts and model diesel in a flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer and a condenser. After setting the reaction temperature at 125 °C, an airflow at 100 mL/min was introduced into the mixture above under stirring at 1000 rpm. The residual sulfur content in model diesel was monitored via gas chromatography (GC, Agilent-7890 A) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID) and a capillary column (HP-5, 30 m × 0.32 mm × 0.25 μm) after extraction by a polar solvent. The extraction was performed following the literature [61]. In a typical method, 20 mL of oxidized model diesel and 5 mL of acetonitrile were charged using a 50 mL separatory funnel, and then the mixture was shaken for 10 min in ambience before the oil and extractant were separated. The sulfur removal (%) was calculated by the formula as follows:
(2)
Sulfur removal =
(
C 0
−
C t
)
/
C 0
×
100 %
here C0 is the initial sulfur content in the model diesel, and Ct is the residual sulfur content in the model diesel after t time of reaction.

The FT-IR spectra, UV–vis DRS spectra and XRD patterns of the as-prepared BCNs were collected to study the structure of BCNs (Fig. 1 ). The peaks in FT-IR spectra at 1380 and 787 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration form of B–N and bending vibration of B–N–B, respectively, indicating the presence of the BN domain in the BCNs [62,63]. Moreover, the absorption bands in UV–vis DRS spectra at 245 nm is assigned to the n electron excitation in the BN domain [64,65]. Notably, another absorption band at 325 could be detected in the UV–vis DRS spectrum of the BCN-1, indicating the presence of delocalized electron [65]. Based on the literature, the doped carbon in the BN matrix can form the conjugate bonding structure with adjacent atoms improving the electron delocalization [31,64,66]. Besides, there are three broad peaks in the XRD patterns of the BCNs. The peaks at 2θ = 24.6°, 42.5°, and 77.8° are assigned to (0002), (10
1 ¯
0) and (11
2 ¯
0), respectively [36,67], indicating the hexagonal crystalline phase of the BCNs. These results indicated that all the as-prepared BCNs have similar hexagonal structures consisting of separated BN and carbon domains.

The electronic micrographs of the as-prepared BCN-1 were exhibited (Fig. 2 ). It could be observed in the SEM image that the BCN-1 possesses a sheet structure. The size of the BCN-1 was in micron-scale, preventing the sheet structure from being stacked. Moreover, the wrinkled edge of the BCN-1 was also observed from the TEM image, indicating the typical morphology of the two-dimensional layered structure.

The porous structure of BCN was investigated by the N2 adsorption-desorption test (Fig. 3 ). It was found that the BCNs showed the typical type-I (B) curves, indicating the presence of a microporous structure [68]. The specific surface areas of the BCN-1, BCN-2, BCN-3 and BN are 1004, 842, 628 and 1237 m2/g, respectively. It is noted that the BCNs have lower specific surface areas than that of the BN, which is ascribed to the different contribution from the various porous structure. Compared with the BN, the BCNs had fewer micropores in the range from 0.8 to 1.2 nm, which were suggested to be formed by the gas generated from the pyrolysis of urea [69]. This abundant microporous structure contributes to the large specific surface area of the samples. On the other hand, another flexible mesoporous structure with a narrow width distribution among 2–4 nm could also be detected only in the BCNs. This unique hierarchical porous structure in the BCNs could be attributed to the template function of DES, which was decomposed and removed during the calcination process. It was suggested that the existence of hierarchical porous structure would be beneficial for the mass transfer and diffusion of substrates [70–75].

Meanwhile, the broad asymmetric band in the N 1s spectrum of the BCN-1 could also be deconvoluted into two bands at 397.8 and 399.0 eV (Fig. 4B), which were assigned to the N–B and N–C bonding structures, respectively [76]. Similar to the B 1s spectrum, the band of N–B bonding structure in the N 1s spectrum of the BCN-1 also shifted to lower binding energy compared with that of the BN (Fig. 4D), indicating the higher electron density on the BCN-1.

Also, it was noted that a redshift on bending vibration was detected in the FT-IR spectra from 808 to 787 cm−1 for the B–N–B bending vibration of BN and as-prepared BCN-1, respectively (Fig. 4E). This phenomenon indicated the decreased stress on the bending vibration of B–N–B in the BCN-1 that could be caused by some electron-donating groups, such as the sp2 carbon. Moreover, an enhanced EPR signal of intrinsic paramagnetic defects at g = 2.003 was detected in the BCN-1, indicating the increase of the unpaired electron in the BCN-1 (Fig. 4F).

The theoretical calculation was employed to study the electronic structure of the BCN. It was found that the BCN has an improved electron delocalization on the highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) than the BN (Fig. S1). This result was consistent with the previous literature report [31]. Meanwhile, the BCN and BN models with unsaturated zigzag edges were constructed and optimized (Fig. S2). The charge distribution on the edges was calculated by natural population analysis (NPA) and listed (Table S2). It was found that there were more negative charges on the zig-N and zig-B edges of the BCN. This phenomenon indicated that the zigzag edges in the BCN are activated. As a result, the BCN-1, which contains the most carbon content, should have the most active edges, and be expected to exhibit high-performance for the oxidation of sulfur compounds.

The desulfurization performance of BCN-1 was tested for the DBT substrate (Fig. 5 A). It could be observed when using BCN-1 as the catalyst, the residual sulfur content in model diesel reduced from 500 to 8 ppm in 4 h at 125 °C. However, the sulfur removal for the BN could barely reach 64.5% under the same reaction conditions. This phenomenon indicated that the specific surface area was not the main factor affecting the reaction rate. The reason for this improved activity on the BCN-1 was investigated by the cyclic voltammetry curves (Fig. 5B). The BCN-1 showed a stronger current density at a more positive reduction potential (0.54 mA/cm2 at 0.57 V) than that of BN (0.35 mA/cm2 at 0.55 V), indicating an improved charge transfer from BCN-1 to dioxygen. Moreover, no other reduction peak could be observed in the curves of BCN-1, which proved that carbon clusters in the BCN would not activate oxygen. Owing to the enhanced oxygen activation process, the BCN-1 catalyzed aerobic oxidative desulfurization had a noticeable improvement.

The reaction temperature is an essential parameter for the catalytic efficiency of the BCN-1 (Fig. 6B). It could be seen that the catalytic reaction was sensitive to temperature changes from 120 to 130 °C. When the reaction was carried out at 130 °C, the DBT removal could reach 100% in 4 h due to an accelerated initial reaction rate. However, the initial reaction rate was severely insufficient when the reaction temperature reduced to 120 °C. This phenomenon indicated that the reaction rate was affected by the mass transfer in the porous structure, which could be improved at a higher temperature.

The DBT derivatives have a higher steric hindrance on the sulfur atom, and they are hard to be removed by the hydrodesulfurization technology under mild conditions. In this work, the BCN-1 could achieve deep sulfur removal for the 4-MDBT and 4,6-DMDBT in 8 h to obtain the clean oil with the sulfur residue of 9 and 8 ppm respectively, exhibiting the potential of the BCN in the ODS technology (Fig. 6C). The reactive selectivity decreased in the order of DBT >4,6-DMDBT > 4-MDBT, correlating well with the theoretical result using Fukui function in our previous work [86].

The stability and cycling performance of the catalyst is another parameter to evaluate the catalytic performance of the catalyst. Thus, the recycling experiment was carried out to simulate intermittent reaction. After the previous round of reaction, the catalyst was separated from the model diesel by a simple decantation method, and the separated catalyst was then transferred into the next round of reaction without regeneration. It could be investigated that the BCN-1 could still have 95% efficiency for the DBT removal after 5 times repeated cycles (Fig. 6D). This result indicated that the hierarchical porous structure of the BCN-1 was stable and not be blocked in the catalytic reaction.

To reach deep learning of the improved catalytic activity on the BCN-1, theoretical calculations combining with possible cluster models of BN and BCN with zig-N or zig-B edge was employed to reveal the mechanism. The catalytic oxidation process could be divided into four parts: the molecular adsorption of oxygen, the dissociation of oxygen, the generation of oxidative product and the recovery of reactive sites (Fig. S3). The IM-BCN-B-A, IM-BCN-N-A, IM-BN-B-A and IM-BN-N-A were the intermediates for the molecular adsorption of dioxygen. The IM-BCN-B-D, IM-BCN-N-D, IM-BN-B-D and IM-BN-N-D were the intermediates for the dissociation of dioxygen. Whereas the IM-BCN-B-P, IM-BCN-N-P, IM-BN-B-P and IM-BN-N-P were the intermediates for the generation of oxidative product. All of the ΔG values of these models were plotted (Fig. 7 ) and listed (Table S3), respectively. For the models with a zig-B edge, it was found that the dioxygen tended to be strongly adsorbed on the zig-B edge, leading to a difficulty in the recovery of reactive sites. This phenomenon could be attributed to strong B–O interaction [87]. Thus, the most active sites could not be on the zig-B edge of BCN-1 despite the indeed improvement on the dioxygen activation [36].

On the other hand, it could be found that dioxygen can also be dissociated on the zig-N edge. Moreover, the reaction on the BCN was through a higher energy pathway than that on the BN, which was beneficial to the desorption of dibenzothiophene sulfone (DBTO2) and recovery of active sites. Besides, compared with the BN, the dissociation energy of dioxygen for the BCN (−126.5 kJ/mol) was higher than that of the BN (−107.3 kJ/mol), indicating the dissociation of dioxygen was much easier on the zig-N edge of BCN. Moreover, the distance between two oxygen atoms in IM-BCN-N-D (2.770 Å) was longer than that of IM-BN-N-D (2.484 Å), which also confirmed the above results. This analysis indicated that the carbon clusters could assist BCN-1 to stabilize the dissociation of dioxygen on the zig-N edge. Meanwhile, the recovery of reactive sites on the zig-N edge of BCN was more facile than BN, which could promote the recovery of reactive sites. In summary, the mechanism of the enhanced oxidative desulfurization activity on BCN-1 was proposed (Scheme 2 ).

In this study, a series of metal-free hierarchical porous BCN catalysts were successfully synthesized using DES as the precursor and template. Using dioxygen as the oxidant, the as-prepared BCN catalyst exhibited impressive catalytic performance for the oxidative desulfurization of diesel. Under the optimized conditions, 98.4% sulfur removal could be achieved in 4 h. Compared with the BN, the as-prepared BCN catalyst had an improved electron delocalization, which promoted the activation of dioxygen on the zig-N edges. This work provides a new approach to synthesize high-performance metal-free catalyst for the aerobic oxidative desulfurization of fuel.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2017YFB0306504), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21722604, 21576122 and 21776116), and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX18_2234).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micromeso.2019.109788.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mesoporous silica as selective sorbents for removal of sulfones from oxidized diesel fuel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Nanoti, S. Dasgupta, A.N. Goswami, B.R. Nautiyal, T.V. Rao, B. Sain, Y.K. Sharma, S.M. Nanoti, M.O. Garg, P. Gupta, Mesoporous silica as selective sorbents for removal of sulfones from oxidized diesel fuel, Micropor. Mesopor. Mater., 124 (2009) 94-99.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidative desulfurization using ordered mesoporous silicas as catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.H. Wang, N. Liu, J.Y. Zhang, X. Zhao, W.H. Zhang, M.H. Zhang, Oxidative desulfurization using ordered mesoporous silicas as catalysts, J. Mol. Catal. A, 393 (2014) 47-55.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyoxometalate-based ionic liquid catalyst with unprecedented activity and selectivity for oxidative desulfurization of diesel in Omin BF4

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.W. Hao, L.L. Sun, T. Su, D.M. Hao, W.P. Liao, C.L. Deng, W.Z. Ren, Y.M. Zhang, H.Y. Lu, Polyoxometalate-based ionic liquid catalyst with unprecedented activity and selectivity for oxidative desulfurization of diesel in Omin BF4, Chem. Eng. J., 358 (2019) 419-426.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molybdenum-containing dendritic mesoporous silica spheres for fast oxidative desulfurization in fuel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, J. Liu, J. Yang, X. Chen, M. Wang, H. Li, W. Zhu, H. Li, Molybdenum-containing dendritic mesoporous silica spheres for fast oxidative desulfurization in fuel, Inorg. Chem. Front., 6 (2019) 451-458.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic POM-based mesoporous silica for fast oxidation of aromatic sulfur compounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, Y.C. Wei, R. Li, W.S. Zhu, H.P. Li, Q. Zhang, M. Wang, X. Chen, H.M. Li, Magnetic POM-based mesoporous silica for fast oxidation of aromatic sulfur compounds, Fuel, 209 (2017) 545-551.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-light induced photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization using BiVO4/C3N4@SiO2 with air/cumene hydroperoxide under ambient conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Miao, D.S. Huang, X.L. Ren, X. Li, Z. Li, J. Xiao, Visible-light induced photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization using BiVO4/C3N4@SiO2 with air/cumene hydroperoxide under ambient conditions, Appl. Catal. B, 192 (2016) 72-79.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid extraction and catalytic oxidative desulfurization of fuels using dialkylpiperidinium tetrachloroferrates catalysts

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene over monoclinic VO2 phase-transition catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Chen, N. Liu, M.H. Zhang, D.H. Wang, Oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene over monoclinic VO2 phase-transition catalysts, Appl. Catal. B, 212 (2017) 32-40.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CoMo/SBA-15 catalysts prepared with EDTA and citric acid and their performance in hydrodesulfurization of dibenzothiophene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Pena, D. Valencia, T. Klimova, CoMo/SBA-15 catalysts prepared with EDTA and citric acid and their performance in hydrodesulfurization of dibenzothiophene, Appl. Catal. B, 147 (2014) 879-887.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidative desulfurization of benzothiophene and thiophene with WOx/ZrO2 catalysts: effect of calcination temperature of catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Hasan, J. Jeon, S.H. Jhung, Oxidative desulfurization of benzothiophene and thiophene with WOx/ZrO2 catalysts: Effect of calcination temperature of catalysts, J. Hazard. Mater., 205 (2012) 216-221.
